title: Homework and the AI Question

modules.title: The Essay That Wrote Itself
modules.sub: One student, one prompt, and a very big dilemma
modules.description: Learners follow a student who uses AI to generate a complete essay overnight and watches things get complicated fast. They explore the immediate payoff and the hidden costs, setting up the core tension the whole course will wrestle with.

modules.title: Shortcut or Cheat? You Decide
modules.sub: Drawing the line between helpful and dishonest AI use
modules.description: Learners examine a set of real-ish scenarios and vote on whether each one counts as a smart shortcut or an act of academic dishonesty. Through debate and reflection, they start to notice that the line isn't always obvious — and that where they draw it actually matters.

modules.title: When AI Helps You Learn More
modules.sub: Discovering how AI can deepen understanding, not replace it
modules.description: Learners experiment with ways of using AI that actively build their knowledge — asking it to explain concepts, challenge their reasoning, or give feedback on a draft they wrote themselves. They compare these approaches to the copy-paste method and reflect on what they actually got out of each.

modules.title: Spotting AI Slop in the Wild
modules.sub: Learning to recognise hollow, AI-generated content on sight
modules.description: Learners read a mix of student writing samples — some human, some AI-generated, some a blend — and try to identify which is which and why. They build a practical eye for the vague, overconfident, or weirdly smooth qualities that often signal low-effort AI output.

modules.title: The Honesty Audit
modules.sub: Reviewing your own AI habits with an honest eye
modules.description: Learners reflect on their current or likely AI use in schoolwork and run it through a simple self-audit framework. They identify moments where AI helped them genuinely learn and moments where it may have done the thinking for them, leaving with a clearer picture of their own patterns.

modules.title: Your Personal AI Use Policy
modules.sub: Writing the rules you will actually stick to
modules.description: Learners draft a short personal policy that defines exactly how they will and won't use AI in their academic work, grounded in everything they've explored across the course. They road-test it against a few tricky edge cases before finalising a version they can genuinely stand behind.

synopsis: Follow a student who discovers AI can finish their homework in seconds — and then has to live with the consequences of every choice they make about how to use it. This course challenges learners to draw their own lines between smart assistance and dishonest shortcuts, leaving with a personal policy they actually believe in.
rationale: With AI homework tools now widely accessible, students urgently need a framework for ethical, effective use before habits form — and that framework must be built through their own reasoning, not handed to them as rules.
learning_outcome: Students can distinguish between AI use that deepens their learning and AI use that bypasses it, and can articulate and defend a personal policy for honest AI assistance in academic work.
source_signals: The AI-Education Death Spiral a.k.a. Let the Kids Cheat
26% of students ages 13-17 are using ChatGPT to help with homework, study finds
AI study buddy: how college students are using AI to learn smarter and faster
How AI study tools support students
His students suddenly started getting A's. Did a Google AI tool go too far?
